page: Google Cloud Platform ct5p-hightpu-1t is a specialized accelerator instance belonging to the ct5p family. It features 52 dedicated x86_64 vCPUs and 112 GB of system memory, yielding a memory-to-vCPU ratio of approximately 2.15 GB per core. The primary hardware differentiator is the inclusion of a single integrated Google TPU accelerator, designed specifically for machine learning workloads.

This instance type does not include any local NVMe SSD storage, requiring users to rely on network-attached storage options for persistent data. Additionally, it provides zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses. The low memory-to-core ratio of 2.15 GB per vCPU represents a potential constraint for memory-intensive applications, positioning the system primarily for compute tasks that offload heavy mathematical operations to the TPU.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the ct5p-hightpu-1t consolidates a high-density dedicated CPU allocation with a specialized TPU accelerator, minimizing the overhead of general-purpose hardware for targeted workloads. This profile is optimized for machine learning model training, deep learning inference, and data-parallel computational tasks that leverage TPU acceleration rather than large system memory footprints.
description: Google Cloud Platform ct5p-hightpu-1t is a specialized accelerator instance configured with 52 dedicated x86_64 vCPUs, 112 GB of system memory, and one integrated Google TPU. With a memory-to-vCPU ratio of approximately 2.15 GB per core, this instance is tailored for compute-heavy tasks that offload mathematical operations to dedicated accelerator hardware. Tradeoffs include the absence of local storage and complimentary public IPv4 addresses, requiring reliance on network-attached storage and external networking configurations. Qualitatively, the instance offers resource density for specialized workloads by combining a high vCPU count with TPU capabilities. It is designed for machine learning model training, deep learning inference, and parallel computing workloads that benefit from TPU acceleration rather than large system memory allocations.
